diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional syncope is one of the three cardinal high-risk symptoms of severe aortic stenosis and predicts sudden cardiac death.
Arrhythmia is a competing cause of exertional syncope and must be excluded with an ECG.
An exertional trigger argues against a benign neurally-mediated faint, which is typically postural or situational.

symptoms.symptom_name: Exertional chest tightness (angina) resolving with rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Demand ischaemia of the hypertrophied ventricle causes exertional angina even with normal coronary arteries.
Exertional chest tightness in an elderly patient must be worked up with ECG and troponin; telehealth cannot exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over three months

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rising left-ventricular filling pressures from the fixed obstruction cause exertional breathlessness — the dyspnoea limb of the symptomatic triad.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpitations / racing heartbeat before the dizzy spell

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of pre-syncopal palpitations makes a primary tachyarrhythmia somewhat less likely but does not exclude it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Harsh ejection systolic murmur loudest in the aortic area, radiating to the carotids

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A carotid-radiating ejection systolic murmur is the hallmark auscultatory sign — but requires a stethoscope and is unavailable in telehealth; sealed clinician finding.

symptoms.symptom_name: Slow-rising, small-volume carotid pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A slow-rising, small-volume pulse indicates a severe, high-gradient obstruction; requires palpation and is unavailable in telehealth.
A narrow pulse pressure reflects a low, fixed stroke volume across the stenotic valve; a clinician-measured sign.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft aortic component of the second heart sound and a fourth heart sound (S4)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A soft/absent A2 indicates a severely calcified immobile valve; an S4 reflects a stiff hypertrophied ventricle — both require auscultation.
A pressure-loaded, hypertrophied left ventricle produces a sustained apex beat; requires palpation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Transthoracic echocardiography — peak transvalvular velocity, mean gradient and valve area

investigation_details.expected_result: Restricted, calcified aortic valve leaflets with a high peak velocity/mean gradient and reduced valve area consistent with severe aortic stenosis
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Severity and the need for valve intervention cannot be established; management drifts without a diagnosis.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Echocardiography is the confirmatory test for aortic stenosis severity; unavailable in telehealth in real time.

investigation_details.expected_result: May show left-ventricular hypertrophy/strain; used to exclude acute coronary syndrome as the cause of the chest tightness
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: A concurrent acute coronary syndrome could be missed.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: ECG and troponin are required to exclude ACS; neither is obtainable via telehealth.

symptom_clusters.cluster_name: Ominous exertional triad of severe aortic stenosis
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Exertional syncope, angina and progressive dyspnoea together mark symptomatic severe aortic stenosis with a sharply worse untreated prognosis and high sudden-death risk.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: To exclude acute coronary syndrome as the cause of the exertional chest tightness — cannot be done via telehealth.
Confirms aortic stenosis and its severity and drives the valve-intervention decision.
Contraindicated in symptomatic severe aortic stenosis — provoking exertion risks syncope, arrhythmia and sudden death; the AI Doctor must not request or endorse it.
